bibliographic_data.title: WhatsApp security and role of metadata in preserving privacy
bibliographic_data.abstract: WhatsApp messenger is arguably the most popular mobile app available on all smartphones. Over one billion people worldwide use it for free messaging, calling, and media sharing. In April 2016, WhatsApp switched to a default end-to-end encrypted service. This means that all messages (SMS), phone calls, videos, audios, and any other form of information exchanged cannot be read by any unauthorized entity since WhatsApp version 2.16.2 (released April 2016). In this paper we analyze the WhatsApp messaging platform and critique its security architecture along with a focus on its privacy preservation mechanisms. We report that the Signal Protocol, which forms the basis of WhatsApp end-to-end encryption, does offer protection against forward secrecy and MITM to a large extent. Finally, we argue that simply encrypting the end-to-end channel cannot preserve privacy. The metadata can reveal just enough information to show connections between people, their patterns, and personal information.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper analyzes the security architecture of WhatsApp and critiques its privacy preservation mechanisms, highlighting that end-to-end encryption alone is not sufficient for full privacy protection due to metadata exposure.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors describe important concepts such as end-to-end encryption (E2EE), Signal Protocol, and curve25519. They then evaluate the security architecture of WhatsApp, including key management, authentication mechanisms, message exchange, and perform a security evaluation.
outcomes: Reported that the Signal Protocol offers protection against forward secrecy and MITM to a large extent.
Argued that simply encrypting the end-to-end channel cannot preserve privacy due to metadata exposure.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper analyzes the security architecture of WhatsApp, focusing on its privacy preservation mechanisms. It highlights that while end-to-end encryption (E2EE) and the Signal Protocol provide strong protection against certain types of attacks, they do not fully protect user privacy due to metadata exposure. The authors describe the importance of understanding key concepts such as E2EE, Signal Protocol, and curve25519 before evaluating the security architecture. They cover the security architecture in detail, including key management, end-to-end encryption, authentication mechanisms, message exchange, and perform a comprehensive security evaluation.
